scenario: A FEEL expression must redact a phone number in a string: replace all digits with X. Input: "Call me at +359 888 123456". Desired output: "Call me at +XXX XXX XXXXXX".
question: Which FEEL built-in fits "regex-based string replacement"?

options.text: replaceAll() method-call syntax reflex with no equivalent in FEEL. FEEL does not support dot-notation method calls on string values, and there is no replaceAll function in the FEEL built-in library. The correct FEEL function for regex-based string replacement is replace(string, pattern, replacement) using standalone function-call syntax: replace(phoneNumber, "[0-9]", "X"). The FEEL replace function uses Java-compatible regex syntax for the pattern parameter and replaces all occurrences of the pattern match in the input string.

It requires splitting the string into individual characters first, applying per-character logic, then re-joining. The built-in replace(string, pattern, replacement) function handles the same task in a single expression: replace(s, "[0-9]", "X"), replacing all regex-matched occurrences efficiently without manual iteration.

replace(string, regex, replacement) -- FEEL's built-in regex-replace function that substitutes all occurrences of the regex pattern match in the string with the replacement value. For example: replace("Call me at +359 888 123456", "[0-9]", "X") returns "Call me at +XXX XXX XXXXXX" by replacing every digit with X. The function uses Java-compatible regex syntax. Backreference groups are supported in the replacement string using $1, $2 notation. The replace function replaces all matches globally, not just the first occurrence, making it equivalent to a global regex replace operation.

regex_replace(s, pattern, val) -- this function name is invented and has no runtime implementation in Camunda 8 in the FEEL built-in function library. There is no regex_replace function in the FEEL specification. The correct FEEL function for regex-based string replacement is replace(string, pattern, replacement): replace("+359 888 123456", "[0-9]", "X") returns "+XXX XXX XXXXXX". The regex_replace naming convention comes from other languages like PHP or Python where regex functions often have regex_ prefixes, but FEEL uses the shorter replace name for its built-in regex substitution function.

a.text: Incorrect. replace(string, regex, replacement) is FEEL's string replacement. The regex matches all occurrences (no need for a global flag -- FEEL replace is globally-acting). The replacement string can include backreferences for capturing groups (if regex includes them). Common use cases: redaction (replace sensitive data), normalisation (strip whitespace), formatting (e.g., format phone numbers). Companion: replace(string, regex, replacement, flags) -- some FEEL implementations support optional flags for case-insensitivity, etc.
